Target type: Corporations (food retailers, fast-food chains, food-service companies) as primary target; a secondary legislative target (UEP-HSUS federal deal) had already collapsed by 2014, triggering the deliberate pivot to corporate campaigns exclusively
Concentration of decision-making power: Moderately concentrated — a relatively small number of large corporate buyers (McDonald's, Walmart, Sodexo, Subway, etc.) held decisive market power; winning the largest buyers created cascade pressure on smaller ones.
Strength of opposition: Moderate — United Egg Producers (representing 95% of domestic egg producers) had real institutional presence and funded a 2015 industry study to undermine the cage-free case, but by 2016 UEP's own president publicly conceded a cage-free future was "a fait accompli."
Type of opposition: Mixed — industry-funded counter-research (the 2015 aviary mortality/air-quality study); public counter-messaging by UEP president; no evidence of legal challenges or political lobbying specifically targeting the corporate campaign (as distinct from earlier legislative resistance)
Visibility of harm to the public: Low-to-moderate at campaign start, actively built — WaPo directly states "fifteen years ago, few Americans other than farmers seemed to be thinking much about hens"; HSUS undercover investigation videos reaching half-a-million views in a day were a deliberate visibility-building tactic
Degree of behaviour change required: Low for corporations at the commitment stage — a policy pledge requiring no immediate operational change; High for egg producers and supply chain over the 10-year implementation horizon (barn conversion, capital expenditure, new equipment, certification
Role of policy vs market pressure vs cul: Tiered: Primary — market pressure (direct corporate negotiation and campaign pressure on buyers); Secondary — cultural shift (foodie culture, public concern for animal welfare building consumer-side demand); Policy played a framing/precedent role (Prop 2, 2008) but was not the primary lever in this window.
Coalition size, type, and presence of no: Narrow but coordinated — a small cluster of animal welfare orgs (HSUS, THL, MFA, CIWF) operating with deliberate single-issue focus and sector-by-sector coordination; Open Philanthropy Project as a non-obvious financial ally providing $2.5M across three orgs to fund the campaign; no broad cross-sector coalition of the kind seen in other cases
Economic disruption required: Moderate — industry-backed study put production costs 36% higher; activists cited 12–15 cents/dozen; barn retrofits and equipment procurement required significant capital expenditure but did not require a full business-model change (egg farming continues, just differently)
Speed of change achieved: High once the cascade began — Sodexo's February 2015 decision triggered a rapid cascade; McDonald's (America's largest egg buyer) followed in September 2015; nearly 200 companies pledged within two years; UEP conceded publicly by 201
Scale of change achieved: National, significant but deferred — nearly 200 companies covering half of 7 billion monthly eggs pledged by end of 2016; however, implementation deadline is 2025, meaning the actual physical transition remained largely incomplete at the window's close; Sodexo's July 2016 global pledge extended reach to 80 countries
Public sentiment starting point: Latently sympathetic. Pre-campaign national survey evidence shows most US consumers perceived caged housing as reducing hen welfare and were willing to pay a premium for cage-free eggs before the 2015–2016 campaign escalation. Concern was present but not yet activated into demand.
Legal/regulatory landscape: Partially supportive precedent, no direct legislative lever — California's Prop 2 (2008) and AB 1437 (2010) created a legal precedent that the campaign cited but did not directly rely on; the 2011 UEP-HSUS federal deal's 2014 collapse is what drove the deliberate pivot to the corporate route; no blocking legislation in-window
Narrative dominance: Contested but shifting — UEP controlled the "eggs are affordable food" and "cage-free isn't actually better for hens" counter-narrative; by 2016 the campaign's framing ("incremental social progress," "welfare improvement for 280 million animals") had achieved significant mainstream media legitimacy, with WaPo framing cage-free as "one of the animal advocacy movement's biggest victories in decades."
Notable tactics:
a. Sector-by-sector sequencing — deliberately targeting one industry sector at a time (food service first, then grocery, then fast food) rather than a broad simultaneous campaign, allowing wins to cascade.
 b. Single-company sustained pressure — maintaining campaigns against individual companies until they pledged, using protests, shaming websites, and investor/client outreach as escalating pressure tools.
 c. Investor and shareholder engagement — SEC-registered exempt solicitation (HSUS re: Kraft) using formal financial regulatory mechanisms rather than just public advocacy.
 d. Database-driven corporate targeting — THL explicitly described maintaining databases of egg-buying companies, systematically tracking and prioritising targets by market share and campaign palatability.
 e. Open Philanthropy/funder coordination — $2.5M in coordinated grants across three orgs (THL, MFA, HSUS) to concentrate resources on a single issue at a decisive moment

Target type: Mixed, corporate-primary. Voluntary corporate commitment (retail/foodservice/manufacturing) is the primary target; EU legislative/regulatory action is an emerging secondary target.
Concentration of decision-making power: Diffuse. 394+ independent corporate decision-makers required across multiple countries and sectors; concentrated only within each firm's own leadership.
Strength of opposition: High. Coordinated EU-wide trade body (AVEC) commissioned a detailed, named-author, methodologically transparent economic counter-study; AVEC president made sustained public statements contesting the welfare framing
Type of opposition: Mixed. Industry economic/scientific counter-study; consumer-choice/affordability reframing; direct corporate walkback (18 UK brand withdrawals, 2025–26).
Visibility of harm to the public: Low-Medium. Broiler welfare harms (stocking density, fast growth) are technical and diffuse; only surfacing via active investigation; no equivalent to battery-cage iconic imagery evidenced in locked sources.
Degree of behaviour change required: High for industry. Requires breed change, stocking density reduction, new housing construction (9,000+ new poultry houses EU-wide), altered growing cycles; no consumer-facing behaviour change required.
Role of policy vs market pressure vs cul: Primarily market-led (voluntary corporate commitments); emerging secondary policy lever (EFSA 2023 opinion informing EU Broiler Directive revision; EU Commission legal complaint process).
Coalition size, type, and presence of no: Broad, international. 95-organisation Open Wing Alliance; non-obvious allies include national certification schemes (Haltungsform, Germany) and at least one producer (Norsk Kylling); coalition explicitly diverse in tactics despite single-issue alignment.
Economic disruption required: High. Industry-modelled +37.5% cost per kg meat, 44% output reduction per m², approximately €8.24–12.9bn EU-wide capital need; independently corroborated by Mostert et al. 2022 life-cycle assessment.
Speed of change achieved: Mixed/Unresolved. Rapid full compliance by early movers (Waitrose, M&S) against severe breed-criterion lag (36 companies under 10% progress, 17 at 0%) and 18 UK withdrawals in 2025–26. Status as of July 2026.
Scale of change achieved: Mixed/Unresolved. 394+ companies, approximately 1.3bn chickens under commitment, 260m+ impacted; full compliance rare; 29 of 93 tracked companies disclosed no progress in 2025; active reversal visible in UK market.
Public sentiment starting point: Latently sympathetic-to-supportive. Eurobarometer October 2023: 84% of Europeans want farmed animal welfare better protected.
Legal/regulatory landscape: Permissive but evolving. No binding EU standard at ECC-level at campaign start; EU Council Directive 2007/43/EC sets a lower floor (42kg/m²) that ECC voluntarily exceeds (30kg/m²); landscape opening via EFSA 2023 opinion, EU Commission legal complaint acknowledgment (2023), and UK judicial review (dismissed on relief sought, not merits).
Narrative dominance: Contested/Mixed. Industry consumer-choice/affordability and sustainability framing (+24.4% GHG emissions, food security risk) actively competing with welfare/science-backed framing; no single narrative dominant as of July 2026.
Notable tactics:
a. Joint single-issue letter despite normal non-alignment among signatory organisations, explicitly acknowledging internal disagreement on other issues and tactics.
 b. Standardised criteria with built-in third-party audit and annual public reporting requirement embedded in the commitment structure itself, not added on.
 c. Public annual compliance scorecard naming individual companies by criterion, sustained over multiple years as ongoing reputational accountability mechanism.
 d. Sector-spanning simultaneous targeting across retailers, food service, restaurants, manufacturers, and producers in parallel rather than sequentially.
 e. Case-by-case roadmap negotiation with individual companies to support implementation beyond the point of signing.
 f. Legal complaint filed with EU Commission as parallel institutional pressure lever running alongside the voluntary commitment campaign.

Target type: Corporation (single, primary target: SeaWorld Entertainment Inc.); secondary government targets (California Coastal Commission, California legislature) emerged mid-campaign via the Blue World permit fight and AB 2140/2305
Concentration of decision-making power: High — single publicly-traded corporation with a small executive/board decision-making unit; secondary target equally concentrated (11-vote Coastal Commission
Strength of opposition: High initially, eroding — SeaWorld entered the window as a large, well-resourced publicly-traded corporation with PR firms, legal counsel, and lobbying capacity; institutional strength visibly weakened over the window as stock collapsed, CEO resigned, and corporate partners defecte
Type of opposition: Multi-form, sequenced — public counter-campaign (open letters, "Blackfish: The Truth" rebuttal, advertising); legal challenge (lawsuit vs. California Coastal Commission); political obstruction (opposing AB 2140 in 2014); shifting to co-optation by 2016 (adopting conservation-education framing, neutral stance on AB 2305)
Visibility of harm to the public: High and rapidly rising — orca attacks and trainer deaths were directly filmed and broadcast nationally; CNN airing reached approximately 21 million viewers within one month; visceral, concrete harm (trainer deaths, dorsal fin collapse) was highly visible
Degree of behaviour change required: Low-Moderate — SeaWorld was required to discontinue one specific practice (breeding) and one show format; core business model (parks, other animal exhibits, ticketed entertainment) continued operating unchanged
Role of policy vs market pressure vs cul: Primary: market and reputational pressure — stock collapse, attendance drop, and corporate partner defections directly drove the corporate decision. Secondary: cultural shift in public perception of captivity. Policy (AB 2140/2305) ran parallel but the corporate decision preceded the law's passage by six months
Coalition size, type, and presence of no: Broad and diverse — animal welfare and rights organisations (PETA, HSUS, AWI, ALDF, Sea Shepherd, IMMP) formed the core; genuine non-obvious allies included independent scientists (Goodall, Marino), former industry insiders (ex-SeaWorld trainers including Hargrove), corporate partners who defected (Southwest Airlines, Miami Dolphins, Seattle Seahawks), and musicians and celebrities (Pamela Anderson, Pat Benatar
Economic disruption required: Low-Moderate — ending the breeding programme and one show format; SeaWorld retained all animals, opened new shows, and continued operating; not a threat to the core captive entertainment business model
Speed of change achieved: High — single decisive corporate announcement (17 March 2016) after approximately three years of sustained pressure; tipped rapidly once Coastal Commission permit conditions and lawsuit pressure converged with stock and reputational damage.
Scale of change achieved: Moderate-High — affected SeaWorld company-wide across all US parks and approximately 22–30 orcas; triggered some international ripple (Vancouver Aquarium 2018) and cross-species precedent (Ringling Bros. elephants); did not end captivity globally or change federal law (ORCA Act never passed in-window)
Public sentiment starting point: Latently opposed but divided. Pre-campaign polling (July 2012, n=1,000+, nationally representative) found only 26% of Americans supported orca captivity, 39% opposed it, and 35% were undecided — a plurality opposition but with a large undecided bloc. Eighty-two percent agreed orca confinement was a convincing reason to end captivity. The campaign entered a public that was already tilted against the practice but lacked sufficient salience or activation to translate opposition into pressure
Legal/regulatory landscape: No federal blocking or enabling legislation; California state-level levers available (Coastal Commission authority, contested but ultimately upheld; AB 2140/2305 legislative pathway) and used; corporate decision preceded formal legal resolution; ORCA Act stalled federally in-window
Narrative dominance: Low initially, rapidly contested and lost by SeaWorld — SeaWorld's "education/conservation" framing was deeply embedded pre-2013 with no real challenger; Blackfish introduced a competing captivity-as-cruelty frame that prevailed in the framing contest within approximately three years per academic framing analysis
Notable tactics:
a. Strategic multi-platform distribution sequencing — Sundance premiere to theatrical release to CNN broadcast to Netflix — deliberately transferring credibility from a trusted news outlet to maximise reach and institutional legitimacy.
 b. Single named subject (Tilikum) as emotional narrative anchor — using one individual orca as protagonist rather than a statistics-led argument to build audience identification and emotional investment.
 c. Coordinated grassroots-to-elite escalation — simultaneous social media campaign (#EmptyTheTanks), celebrity recruitment, and formal legal/regulatory intervention (ALDF/Sea Shepherd motion to intervene in the Coastal Commission case) operating as two documented tiers of one campaign.
 d. Leveraging an unrelated regulatory process as legal leverage — attaching a breeding ban condition to SeaWorld's Blue World tank-expansion permit application through the California Coastal Commission, converting a routine planning process into a campaign pressure point.
 e. Recruiting credible insider defectors — former SeaWorld trainers (including Hargrove, who testified at the Coastal Commission hearing) deployed to break the industry's messaging monopoly from within.
 f. Independent scientist open letter to confer third-party legitimacy — named scientists (Goodall, Marino, Frohoff, Spong, Cousteau) signing a public letter ahead of a contested vote, shifting the credibility balance against SeaWorld's expert claims

Target type: Government (legislative) — federal Congress and the Executive as the formal decision-makers; no corporate or market target involved.
Concentration of decision-making power: Highly concentrated — required votes from the Chamber of Deputies, Senate, and a majority of 32 state congresses, with the actual legislative threshold being a relatively small number of institutional bodies, not millions of individual actors.
Strength of opposition: Low — no organised, named opposition bloc documented during the constitutional-amendment phase itself; opposition only becomes visible later, in secondary-legislation stalling.
Type of opposition: Mixed — none during passage (unanimous votes at every stage); post-passage, alleged industry-linked legislative inertia (102 stalled initiatives) during secondary-law drafting.
Visibility of harm to the public: Moderate-to-high — animal cruelty in Mexico is a widely acknowledged social issue (cited statistic: Mexico ranked third globally, first in Latin America, for animal mistreatment), giving the campaign a pre-existing visibility base to build on.
Degree of behaviour change required: Low for the constitutional reform itself — required a values/principles-level legislative commitment, not behaviour change by any specific industry; high degree of change deferred to the still-pending General Law.
Role of policy vs market pressure vs cul: Tiered: Primary — policy/legal (constitutional amendment establishing federal legislative authority); Secondary — cultural shift (explicit "paradigm shift" framing emphasised by NGOs and legislators alike).
Coalition size, type, and presence of no: Broad, multi-sector, with cross-party alliance — three Mexican NGOs (Igualdad Animal, HSI/Mexico, Movimiento Conciencia) coordinated jointly; passed with support from five distinct parliamentary groups (Morena, PAN, MC, PRD, PT) — a notably non-polarised cross-party coalition for Mexican politics.
Economic disruption required: Low at the constitutional level — symbolic/framework change carries minimal direct economic cost; economic disruption is deferred entirely to the not-yet-passed General Law, where it is anticipated to be significant for industrial livestock.
Speed of change achieved: Very High — three years from first formal proposal (Dec 2021) to full passage and entry into force (Dec 2024), with the final 10-month sprint (Feb–Nov 2024) compressing committee approval, both chambers' unanimous votes, and 17+ state ratifications.
Scale of change achieved: National, foundational — alters the federal Constitution itself (the highest legal instrument), explicitly compared by academic commentary to a small group of countries (Germany, Switzerland, Ecuador, Bolivia) with constitutional animal-protection provisions; scale of practical effect remains incomplete pending the General Law.
Public sentiment starting point: Latently sympathetic, with growing awareness. Pre-campaign evidence shows Mexican consumers expressed strong interest in farm animal welfare, high empathy for animal needs, and majority demand for more regulation and information, with 99% opposing dogfighting and 86% rejecting animals in entertainment. Concern was rising but not yet organised into demand for constitutional-level protection.
Legal/regulatory landscape: Previously blocked/absent, now affirmatively enabling — prior Supreme Court jurisprudence (cited in academic commentary) explicitly held the Constitution contained no mandate for animal protection beyond wildlife under the environmental-rights clause; the reform directly closes that gap by creating express constitutional authority.
Narrative dominance: Low at campaign start, becoming dominant within the legislative process — no contested counter-narrative is documented at any stage; the lack of any recorded "No" vote across multiple legislative bodies indicates an unusually weak or absent opposing narrative by the time of passage.
Notable tactics:
a. Sustained multi-year petition/signature campaign (140,000+ signatures cited) maintained continuously from 2021 through 2024 rather than a single concentrated push.
 b. Direct, sustained legislator engagement at sub-national level (e.g. Guerrero State Forum) to build provincial buy-in ahead of the eventual state-ratification requirement.
 c. Public demonstration actions timed to coincide with the final legislative votes (gathering at national monuments in Mexico City, Sept 2024).
 d. Explicit reliance on a change in head of state (Sheinbaum publicly committing to the reform as one of "100 top commitments" on her first day) as a tactical lever to convert years of groundwork into final legislative action.
 e. Coalition tactic of multiple NGOs jointly authoring and submitting the same legislative proposal rather than competing with separate bills, reducing legislative fragmentation.

Campaign: Sinergia Animal Global South cage-free corporate campaign (2018–present)
Target type: Corporations (food retailers, manufacturers, fast-food chains, and pork producers/integrators) across multiple Global South countries — diffuse target type, but consistently corporate rather than government, at this stage of the campaign
Concentration of decision-making power: Diffuse-to-moderate — dozens to hundreds of individual companies per country must each independently commit; concentration varies by country (Brazil's pig sector concentrated among ~9 large producers; Latin American egg sector more fragmented across many retailers)
Strength of opposition: Low-to-moderate, fragmented — no organised industry-wide opposition bloc; resistance is company-by-company and largely passive (non-response, slow implementation) rather than active counter-campaigning
Type of opposition: Predominantly passive/non-compliance — silent refusal (Carozzi, multi-year holdout), non-response to monitoring surveys (roughly half of companies contacted), and slow operational transition citing cost/supply constraints; no evidence of organised lobbying or public counter-campaign
Visibility of harm to the public: Low-to-moderate, deliberately built rather than pre-existing — battery cages and gestation crates were not widely visible public concerns in target countries at campaign start; visibility was actively constructed through undercover investigations and awareness campaigns
Degree of behaviour change required: Moderate — requires companies to change supplier relationships and sourcing standards; does not require mass consumer behaviour change, though full pig-welfare reform requires production-process changes more disruptive than cage-free egg sourcing
Role of policy vs market pressure vs cul: Tiered: Primary — market pressure (direct corporate negotiation/campaigns); Secondary — policy (post-2020 Brazilian federal pig-welfare guidelines), used as a foundation for further corporate pressure rather than the primary lever
Coalition size, type, and presence of no: Multi-country coalition with non-obvious allies — built via the international Open Wing Alliance network, country-specific local lawyer/legal volunteer networks, and (per Source #6) explicit cross-border engagement with EU trade-policy debates as an unusual non-obvious lever
Economic disruption required: Low-to-moderate — companies bear sourcing/supply-chain costs (cage-free eggs, crate-free housing) but not a fundamental industry business-model change; cost and supply availability are repeatedly cited by companies as the main friction points
Speed of change achieved: Mixed/Unresolved — rapid early wins in some companies (immediate-implementation policies, five-month campaigns) contrasted with multi-year unresolved holdouts (Carozzi, 2017–2020+) and slow monitored implementation (Brazil pig sector, 2020–2023 incremental progress)
Scale of change achieved: Mixed/Unresolved — broad multi-country reach (8+ countries, hundreds of corporate commitments, hundreds of millions of animals nominally affected) but enforcement/verification remains partial, with roughly half of monitored companies not responding to accountability surveys as of the most recent data
Public sentiment starting point: Insufficient evidence in locked source set — no source directly measures public sentiment in target countries prior to campaign start; case runs on 13 variables for this entry
Legal/regulatory landscape: Initially largely absent, partially strengthened in-window — most target countries had no specific battery-cage or gestation-crate regulation at campaign start; Brazil introduced federal pig-welfare guidelines in 2020 (with a 2045 compliance deadline), creating only a long-horizon legal backstop rather than near-term enforceable protection
Narrative dominance: Latently sympathetic-to-supportive across Sinergia's primary markets. Pre-campaign evidence shows Brazilians had the most pro-animal attitudes of all BRIC countries — 89% believing animals used for meat should be well cared for, 70% supporting laws requiring humane treatment, and 79% believing farmed animals are already treated poorly. Brazilian consumers demonstrated preference for cage-free and free-range systems when informed of production practices. South American consumers across Argentina, Chile, Colombia and other target countries similarly showed strong agreement that farm animal welfare should be safeguarded. A high-meat-consumption cultural context coexisted with this latent sympathy — public concern existed but awareness of specific production practices remained low, making it activatable through investigation and media outreach.
Notable tactics:
a. Pre-launch private notification to target companies before public campaign launch, allowing quiet compliance and avoiding cost of a full public campaign.
 b. Undercover supply-chain investigations tracing a retailer's branded product back to its actual supplying farm, including geolocation evidence.
 c. Annual public scorecard/ranking of named companies (Cage-Free Tracker; Pigs in Focus / Porcos em Foco) sustained over multiple years to enable accountability tracking.
 d. Leveraging membership in an international coalition (Open Wing Alliance) for shared tactics, training, and cross-country learning rather than building campaign infrastructure from scratch in each new country.
 e. Strategic sequencing of issues within a single country — explicitly delaying broiler chicken campaigns until egg-laying hen and pig commitments are closer to implementation, to preserve credibility and capacity

Campaign: UK live export ban Animal Welfare (Livestock Exports) Act (2017–2024)
Target type: Government (primary) — a single national government (Great Britain: UK/England, Scotland, Wales) as the legislative decision-maker; the live export industry and livestock traders as secondary compliance targets once the ban passed
Concentration of decision-making power: High — a single national government with a working parliamentary majority for most of the window; key decisions concentrated in Defra ministerial level with COAG-equivalent devolved government agreement required from Scotland and Wales.
Strength of opposition: Low-to-moderate — no large, well-funded industry counter-campaign evidenced; opposition came primarily from farming unions (NFU, National Sheep Association, Farmers Union of Wales) and a small number of individual exporters; no equivalent to a multinational-funded lobbying operation
Type of opposition: Sectoral and fiscal — farming union opposition focused on economic impact on sheep and cattle farmers dependent on export markets; no legal challenge, no astroturf front group, no public counter-campaign; opposition was concentrated in parliamentary submissions and trade press commentar
Visibility of harm to the public: Moderate-to-high — live export welfare harms were periodically visible through documented incidents (Ramsgate 2012, historical footage of overcrowded trucks and exhausted animals) and sustained media coverage over decades; less visceral than a single iconic event but accumulated visibility was high among the British public
Degree of behaviour change required: Low for the general public; moderate for the industry — the ban required livestock exporters and some farming businesses to redirect animals to domestic slaughter; no mass public behaviour change required; the ban was structural and statutory
Role of policy vs market pressure vs cul: Policy-dominant — legislative change was the sole mechanism; no voluntary industry commitment or market pressure drove the outcome; cultural shift (declining tolerance for live export welfare harms) was a pre-existing enabling condition rather than a campaign tool
Coalition size, type, and presence of no: Broad, cross-party, within animal welfare sector — 40+ organisations in the Better Deal for Animals coalition (RSPCA, CIWF, HSI UK, World Horse Welfare, Animal Aid, FOUR PAWS UK, Humane League UK, PETA UK, and others); cross-party parliamentary support was a significant non-obvious structural ally (Conservative manifesto commitment meant government itself became an unlikely ally); no corporate or non-animal-welfare sector allies identified
Economic disruption required: Low-to-moderate — the live export trade had already declined substantially (from 752,000 animals in 2000 to 43,000 in 2016, and zero since 2020 due to Brexit trade disruption); the ban formalised a near-defunct trade rather than disrupting a thriving industry; modest disruption for remaining sheep farmers with export-dependent business models
Speed of change achieved: Slow overall, then resolved — seven years from manifesto commitment (June 2017) to ban in force (20 May 2024); substantial delay caused by Brexit legislative complexity, the Kept Animals Bill collapse (2022), and re-tabling as a standalone Bill (2023–24); once re-tabled as a standalone Bill in December 2023 passage was rapid (five months to Royal Assent
Scale of change achieved: National, complete within scope — all live exports of cattle, sheep, pigs, goats, and horses from Great Britain for slaughter and fattening banned permanently; Great Britain joined a growing global movement (New Zealand, Brazil); Northern Ireland explicitly excluded due to Windsor Framework constraints
Public sentiment starting point:
Strongly and broadly sympathetic. Pre-campaign Eurobarometer data (2016) found 82% of Europeans — including the UK — believed farmed animal welfare should be better protected. The RSPCA's own 2016 EU referendum briefing cites this figure and documents decades of UK public concern about live export welfare harms. The YouGov poll commissioned by the Better Deal for Animals coalition (February 2019, early in the campaign window) found 81% of British respondents preferred animal welfare laws to be maintained or extended post-Brexit — confirming the sympathetic starting point. The 2020 Defra public consultation found 87% of 11,000+ respondents agreed animals should not be exported for slaughter or fattening (flagged: self-selected respondent pool, not a representative survey). The Lord Douglas-Miller's own second reading speech explicitly noted the government was "a nation of animal lovers" — confirming campaign actors' assessment of the pre-existing public sentiment.
 Confidence: High — multiple independent pre-campaign and early-window sources converge on a strongly sympathetic starting point; Eurobarometer 2016 is the strongest pre-window baseline; YouGov February 2019 provides the most directly relevant in-window representative poll; Defra 2020 consultation figure included and flagged as self-selected.
 Source Tag: RSPCA EU Referendum Briefing 2016 (new addition for Variable 12); McCulloch 2019 (Source 1, locked — YouGov February 2019 figure); Source 10 (Defra 2020 consultation — flagged as self-selected); Source 9 (Lords second reading — Lord Douglas-Miller quote
Legal/regulatory landscape: EU membership was the historic legal blocker; Brexit removed it — the UK was legally prevented from banning live exports under EU free trade rules until Brexit; the 2017 manifesto commitment was explicitly enabled by leaving the EU. Post-Brexit, no WTO or domestic legal obstacle blocked the ban, though WTO rules were raised in parliamentary debate as a theoretical risk and assessed as manageable under the public morals exemption. The ban had positive legal grounding: the government's own 2020 consultation, FAWC scientific opinion, and cross-party parliamentary support all confirmed the legal pathway was clear
Narrative dominance: High and largely uncontested — the "nation of animal lovers" and welfare-harm framing dominated parliamentary debate, media coverage, and public discourse throughout the window; the farming opposition's economic framing was acknowledged but never achieved mainstream resonance given the trade's near-collapse pre-ban; Lord Douglas-Miller's own ministerial speech opened by invoking the welfare narrative, indicating the government had fully adopted the campaign's framing
Notable tactics:
a. Brexit window exploitation — the campaign identified and rapidly mobilised around the specific legislative opportunity created by Brexit, reframing Brexit from a threat to animal welfare into a vehicle for a ban that was impossible under EU membership.
 b. Cross-party manifesto commitment secured — the campaign successfully embedded a live export ban commitment in the 2017 Conservative manifesto, converting the government itself into a de facto ally and creating a political accountability mechanism for delivery.
 c. One million signature petition coalition — CIWF coordinated a joint petition with 38 Degrees, Avaaz, and SumOfUs, delivering over one million signatures to Defra in October 2018; the coalition of normally non-aligned organisations (animal welfare, general civic, global) presented unified public pressure at a critical early-campaign moment.
 d. Standalone Bill strategy after omnibus Bill collapse — when the Kept Animals Bill (which included the live export ban) collapsed in 2022 amid broader political turmoil, the campaign successfully lobbied for the measure to be re-tabled as a standalone Bill, preventing the ban from being lost with the wider legislation.
 e. Scientific expert body mobilisation — the campaign leveraged the FAWC's independent 2019 Opinion recommending animals be transported only when necessary as an authoritative, government-commissioned scientific mandate that the legislation directly responded to, providing non-campaign legitimacy to the core ask

Target type: Government (single, primary) — the New Zealand Labour-led government as the legislative decision-maker, specifically Cabinet and the Minister of Agriculture; the live export industry and livestock exporters as secondary regulatory compliance targets
Concentration of decision-making power: High — Cabinet made the decisive announcement on 14 April 2021; the Animal Welfare Amendment Bill passed through a single-chamber Parliament; decision-making concentrated in a small executive core with a working parliamentary majority; the select committee stage was the only point of meaningful distributed scrutiny
Strength of opposition: Moderate — Federated Farmers, Beef + Lamb NZ, and the live export industry mounted organised opposition through the select committee process and parliamentary debate; National and ACT voted against the Bill at third reading; no equivalent to a multinational-funded counter-campaign during the campaign window itself
Type of opposition: Sectoral/fiscal — farming unions and exporters framed opposition around economic loss to rural communities ($261M annual value cited by ACT); National proposed a regulatory improvement alternative rather than outright opposition; no legal challenge, no astroturf campaign, no PR operation during the campaign window
Visibility of harm to the public: High and event-driven — two acute, high-visibility events within the campaign window dramatically amplified public awareness: the April 2019 ABC exposé of NZ cattle suffering on Sri Lankan farms, and the September 2020 Gulf Livestock 1 sinking killing 5,867 NZ cattle and 41 crew including two New Zealanders; visibility was episodic rather than sustained
Degree of behaviour change required: Low for the general public; moderate for industry — the ban required livestock exporters and farmers with export-dependent business models to redirect animals to domestic slaughter; a 24-month transition period was provided; no mass public behaviour change required
Role of policy vs market pressure vs cul: Policy-dominant — legislative change was the sole mechanism; Cabinet decision triggered the Bill; no voluntary industry commitment or market pressure drove the outcome; the ABC exposé and Gulf Livestock 1 disaster accelerated the cultural and political conditions for the policy decision but the mechanism was statutory
Coalition size, type, and presence of no: Moderate, animal-welfare-sector dominant — SAFE led the campaign with SPCA as the primary coalition partner; other animal welfare organisations (HUHA, World Animal Protection, Veterinarians for Animal Welfare Aotearoa) participated; non-obvious allies included former Chief Veterinary Officer Dr John Hellstrom (industry insider turned opponent) and Auckland Council (intervened to prevent Cornwall Park cattle export in 2021); no corporate or cross-sector allies identified
Economic disruption required: Low-to-moderate — the live export trade had declined substantially by the campaign window; cattle exports for breeding were valued at approximately $261M–$374M annually in 2021–2022 affecting up to 2,900 farms; the MPI Regulatory Impact Statement confirmed the ban would affect a small proportion of the primary sector; no threat to NZ's primary export industries overall
Speed of change achieved: Moderate — April 2019 ABC exposé triggered MPI review; September 2020 Gulf Livestock 1 accelerated government commitment; April 2021 Cabinet announcement; September 2022 Bill passage; April 2023 ban in force — approximately four years from campaign trigger to implementation, with the final legislative phase (18 months from Cabinet decision to commencement) being relatively fast for a major statutory change
Scale of change achieved: National, world-first — New Zealand became the first country in the world to ban all live export of cattle, sheep, goats, and deer by sea (including for breeding and fattening, not just slaughter); the ban covered the full remaining trade without carve-outs; triggered international recognition and influenced subsequent bans in Australia, Brazil, and the E
Public sentiment starting point: Insufficient evidence in locked source set. No representative pre-April 2019 polling specifically measuring NZ general public attitudes toward live exports exists. The Frontiers 2026 peer-reviewed paper explicitly confirms no prior NZ-specific public survey on live exports predates their 2023 study. MPI review submissions (3,500+ in 2019, majority pro-ban; 81% of 2020 review submissions pro-ban) are self-selected respondent pools and do not qualify as representative general public sentiment data per the variable definition. Case runs on 13 variables
Legal/regulatory landscape: Pre-existing partial regulatory framework, with legislative gap closed by the ban — the Animal Welfare (Export of Livestock for Slaughter) Regulations 2016 already prohibited slaughter exports; the campaign targeted the remaining breeding/fattening loophole. No international trade law obstacle was identified as blocking the ban; the MPI Regulatory Impact Statement assessed the ban as legally implementable. The Bill mechanism (prohibiting issuance of animal welfare export certificates) was a clean legislative instrument without constitutional contestation
Narrative dominance: High and campaign-dominant — the welfare-harm narrative was rapidly and decisively established by the ABC exposé and reinforced by the Gulf Livestock 1 disaster; industry's economic framing never achieved mainstream resonance; Minister O'Connor's third reading speech framed the ban explicitly as upholding NZ's animal welfare reputation; National and ACT opposition was confined to parliamentary record and did not achieve public narrative traction
Notable tactics:
a. Media exposé exploitation as campaign trigger — SAFE rapidly mobilised around the April 2019 ABC investigative report, launching a petition and formal MPI submission campaign within weeks, converting a journalism event into a structured political pressure campaign before public attention could dissipate.
 b. Disaster event amplification — the Gulf Livestock 1 sinking (September 2020) was immediately used by SAFE and SPCA to demand a full ban, converting a shipping tragedy into renewed legislative pressure at a moment when MPI was already conducting its Heron review; the campaign framed the disaster as systemic rather than accidental.
 c. Insider defector deployment — former Chief Veterinary Officer and National Animal Welfare Advisory Committee chair Dr John Hellstrom publicly opposed the ban reversal and launched the 2024 parliamentary petition; within the campaign window, the SPCA and SAFE consistently deployed veterinary and scientific expert voices to counter the industry's "gold standard" framing.
 d. Mass submission campaign to government review — SAFE explicitly mobilised supporters to submit to the 2019 MPI review, contributing to 3,500+ submissions overwhelmingly supporting a ban; the volume of public submissions was subsequently cited as evidence of public demand in the legislative process.
 e. Auckland Council intervention — when Cornwall Park Trust planned to export cattle in early 2021, SAFE and SPCA directly contacted Auckland Council, the Trust, and relevant Ministers; the outcry resulted in the cattle not being exported — a concrete mid-campaign win that demonstrated the coalition's capacity to intervene at the local government level and generated media coverage at a critical pre-announcement moment

Campaign: UK foie gras import ban campaign (2021–present)
Target type: Government (UK, primary) — successive UK governments (Conservative 2017–2024, Labour 2024–present) as the legislative decision-maker on import regulation; the EU as a secondary structural obstacle given trade alignment dynamics
Concentration of decision-making power: High — decision rests with a single national government and ultimately a small number of ministers (Defra Secretary of State); no mass public behaviour change or diffuse corporate sign-up required; the blocking mechanism has been internal government resistance and trade negotiation priorities, not parliamentary oppositio
Strength of opposition: Moderate, structurally embedded — no large-scale funded counter-campaign; opposition comes from the French foie gras industry (Cifog) lobbying via diplomatic channels (French Ambassador), internal UK cabinet resistance, and EU trade deal negotiation dynamics; opposition is institutional rather than mass-mobilised
Type of opposition: Diplomatic/trade-structural — French government and Cifog lobbying through trade representatives; internal UK cabinet resistance (described as "backbench rebellion" under Conservatives, and EU deal trade-off under Labour); no public counter-campaign, no legal challenge, no astroturf operation
Visibility of harm to the public: Moderate-High — Animal Equality's investigations (10+ farm exposés) and celebrity advocacy have raised visibility substantially since 2017; force-feeding is viscerally comprehensible once shown; however less than 10% of Brits consume foie gras, limiting personal stakes and mainstream media attention outside campaign spikes
Degree of behaviour change required: Low for the public; none for industry domestically — the ban requires only a regulatory import prohibition; no industry restructuring, no consumer behaviour change mandated; the disruption falls entirely on a small luxury import trade worth approximately €1.7M annually to French producers
Role of policy vs market pressure vs cul: Policy-dominant — the campaign's sole mechanism is legislative import prohibition; market pressure has produced some voluntary retailer withdrawals (Fortnum & Mason 2021, parliamentary estate discussions) but these are secondary; cultural shift is an enabling condition rather than the mechanism
Coalition size, type, and presence of no: Moderate, with notable non-obvious allies — Animal Equality UK leads; coalition includes CIWF, PETA UK, FOUR PAWS, Viva!, Humane World for Animals UK; non-obvious allies include Conservative MPs and CAWF (pressuring their own government), cross-party parliamentary groupings (APPG on Animal Welfare), Michelin-starred chef Alexis Gauthier (industry insider turned advocate), and King Charles (foie gras banned from all royal residences November 2022)
Economic disruption required: Negligible — foie gras imports to the UK total approximately 200 tonnes annually worth ~€1.7M to French producers; an infinitesimal fraction of UK-EU trade; no domestic industry disruption; the economic argument runs in favour of the ban (no domestic producers affected)
Speed of change achieved: Mixed/Unresolved — seven years from campaign launch with no legislation passed; the campaign secured a Conservative manifesto commitment (2017), Action Plan inclusion (2021), Labour pre-election pledge (June 2024), but each commitment was subsequently dropped or stalled; as of June 2026 the Labour government has abandoned the pledge to secure an EU trade dea
Scale of change achieved: Mixed/Unresolved — no legislative change achieved; significant political commitments secured and then reversed; voluntary retailer withdrawals (Fortnum & Mason, parliamentary estate); King Charles royal residence ban (2022) as symbolic non-legislative win; 315,000+ petition signatures; cross-party parliamentary support established but not converted to legislation
Public sentiment starting point:
Strongly and consistently supportive, though flagged. The only available baseline is a YouGov poll conducted 6–7 June 2017 (n=2,079, commissioned by Animal Equality) — at campaign launch, not before it — showing 77% support for an import ban among those who expressed an opinion. This is flagged as at-window-open rather than pre-window; it may reflect early campaign activation rather than a pure baseline. A December 2018 RSPCA/YouGov poll found 67% of the British public wanted lower-welfare food imports (including foie gras) banned. Less than 10% of Brits consume foie gras, suggesting low personal salience but high abstract moral concern.
 Confidence: Medium — directionally consistent but the June 2017 poll is campaign-commissioned and at-window-open; no independent pre-2017 polling on this specific issue found
Legal/regulatory landscape: Permissive post-Brexit but complicated by EU trade alignment — UK domestic production banned since 2006; EU membership previously prevented a unilateral import ban under free trade rules; Brexit removed that barrier in principle; however EU-UK trade negotiations have reintroduced the obstacle, with the EU's food standards alignment rules effectively preventing the UK from banning EU food products on animal welfare grounds without a specific carve-out; WTO public morals exemption theoretically available but untested in this context
Narrative dominance: Mixed/Unresolved — the "if it's too cruel to produce here, it's too cruel to import" framing has achieved high public resonance and cross-party parliamentary adoption; government actors (both Conservative and Labour) have repeatedly acknowledged the cruelty publicly while failing to act; the French industry's "freedom of choice" counter-narrative has not achieved public traction; however the EU trade deal framing has introduced a new competing narrative (economic integration vs animal welfare) that has successfully blocked action as of June 2026
Notable tactics:
a. "Too cruel to produce, too cruel to import" framing — a single, memorable, legally-grounded slogan that makes the hypocrisy of the current position immediately visible and difficult to defend publicly; directly references the Animal Welfare Act 2006 as its logical foundation.
 b. Investigative footage deployment — Animal Equality's 10+ farm investigations in France and Spain, with footage released at strategic campaign moments (including November 2023 exclusive never-before-seen footage) to sustain media coverage and public attention between political milestones.
 c. Celebrity and insider recruitment — systematic engagement of high-profile figures (Ricky Gervais, Miriam Margolyes, Joanna Lumley, Mark Rylance, Peter Egan) alongside industry insiders (Michelin-starred chef Alexis Gauthier replacing foie gras with plant-based faux gras at his restaurants) to generate earned media and cross-demographic credibility.
 d. Petition escalation with staged Downing Street hand-ins — two major public hand-ins (225,000 signatures September 2021; 315,000+ signatures March 2026) timed to political pressure moments, with allied organisations (PETA UK, FOUR PAWS, Viva!) joining to broaden the visible coalition.
 e. Cross-party parliamentary embedding — securing Early Day Motion signatories (31 MPs, 2017–19), Westminster Hall debate (October 2023), open letters from Conservative MPs pressuring their own government, and Labour pre-election commitment — building a parliamentary accountability record across multiple parliaments

Target type: Government (federal legislature), primary — the Australian Parliament as the decision-maker on marriage law; the general Australian public as the secondary target of the postal survey mobilisation campaign (the Yes campaign needed a supermajority Yes result to compel parliamentary action)
Concentration of decision-making power: Dual concentration — the postal survey result needed to move a small number of key federal politicians (Turnbull government cabinet and Coalition backbenchers), while simultaneously requiring mass public mobilisation to produce a compelling Yes result; the final legislative decision rested with a parliamentary free vote of 226 MPs and Senators
Strength of opposition: High — well-resourced, institutionally embedded opposition from the Catholic Church, Australian Christian Lobby, and Coalition for Marriage; legally challenged but failed to block the survey (High Court challenge dismissed 7 September 2017); opposition had decades of institutional infrastructure and access to federal government support
Type of opposition: Multi-form — formal No campaign via Coalition for Marriage (TV advertising, door-knocking, social media); legal challenge (Wilkie v Commonwealth, dismissed); religious institutional mobilisation (Catholic Church, Australian Christian Lobby, Anglican Diocese); parliamentary blocking (Senate initially blocked the compulsory plebiscite bill); subsidiary reframing campaigns (religious freedom, parental rights, freedom of speech).
Visibility of harm to the public: Low pre-campaign for the general public — marriage inequality was a policy abstraction for most Australians; the ACON/CSRH 2016 evidence review explicitly notes that harm to LGBTIQ people was not widely understood. The Yes campaign had to simultaneously make the case for change and make harm visible, against a backdrop of majority latent support that had not been activated into urgent demand
Degree of behaviour change required: Low for the general public in the survey context (return a postal form) but High in terms of cultural norm shift required — the campaign needed to move millions of latently supportive but potentially passive Australians from indifference to active participation, and to hold persuadable voters against a sustained No campaign
Role of policy vs market pressure vs cul: Tiered — primary: cultural shift (changing sufficient public opinion to produce a compelling Yes result); secondary: policy (the Yes result then compelled a parliamentary free vote); market pressure absent as a mechanism
Coalition size, type, and presence of no: Broad and diverse — Australian Marriage Equality and Equality Campaign as campaign leads; non-obvious allies included: corporate sector (Qantas CEO Alan Joyce, ANZ Bank rainbow ATMs, major corporations publicly backing Yes), ACT Government and City of Sydney as institutional allies, and a cross-party parliamentary cohort including Coalition members defying their own leadership
Economic disruption required: None — marriage equality required no economic restructuring, industry change, or resource reallocation; the campaign's economic argument was the reverse (marriage equality as economically positive)
Speed of change achieved: Fast once survey announced — 99 days from survey announcement (9 August) to result (15 November 2017); legislation passed within three weeks of the result (7 December 2017); total window of four months from start to royal assent
Scale of change achieved: National, complete — federal legislation changing the Marriage Act 1961 to remove gender requirements; applied uniformly across all Australian states and territories; 79.5% participation rate; 61.6% Yes result; described by ABS as world-leading in survey innovation and participation
Public sentiment starting point:
Strongly and consistently supportive. Pre-campaign polling shows sustained majority support for marriage equality across multiple years and methodologies: HILDA Survey longitudinal data (2015) found 66% of Australians supported equal rights for same-sex couples; July 2017 Essential Media poll (directly pre-window) found 63% support, 25% opposed, 12% don't know; March 2016 Essential Media found 64% support, 26% opposed. Support had trended upward from 39.8% in 2005 to 66% in 2015. The ACON/CSRH 2016 evidence review explicitly frames the pre-existing public sympathetic environment as the context the campaign was entering. The challenge was not building support from scratch but activating latent majority support into high participation in a voluntary postal survey.
 Confidence: High — multiple independent pre-window sources across longitudinal survey data, commercial polling, and campaign-actor assessment converge on a clear, consistent, well-evidenced starting point
Legal/regulatory landscape: Legally contested but ultimately permissive — the Commonwealth Marriage Act 1961 (as amended in 2004 to define marriage as between a man and a woman) was the legislative target; the High Court dismissed challenges to the survey's legality (7 September 2017), clearing the path; no constitutional barrier to marriage equality was identified; the survey was non-binding, making the legal pathway contingent on parliamentary action post-result
Narrative dominance: High for the Yes campaign in mainstream and corporate arenas; contested in religious and conservative media — the Yes campaign's "love is love" and equality framing dominated mainstream media, corporate communications, and urban public space; the No campaign's religious freedom and parental rights framing found traction in conservative religious communities and regional areas, as evidenced by the geographic distribution of No votes, but did not achieve mainstream narrative dominance
Notable tactics:
a. Corporate sector mobilisation as public legitimacy signal — enlisting major Australian corporations (Qantas, ANZ, others) to publicly support Yes, converting the campaign from a minority-rights issue into a mainstream business and community values issue and providing institutional legitimacy beyond the LGBTIQ community.
 b. Framing the survey as "just a letter" — the Yes campaign's strategy of normalising participation by reducing the ask to a simple postal action ("it's just a letter, put it in the box"), counteracting the deliberate complexity and unfamiliarity of the postal mechanism.
 c. Digital-first field organising via NationBuilder — the Yes campaign used NationBuilder to coordinate a digital volunteer mobilisation infrastructure that enabled decentralised, nationally scalable doorknocking, phone banking, and social media campaigning at a speed not previously seen in Australian civil society campaigns.
 d. Health evidence pre-positioning — ACON and allied organisations published and submitted the Kolstee/Hopwood evidence review (September 2016) to the Senate inquiry on postal survey arrangements, pre-arming the campaign with peer-reviewed evidence of harm from the debate process itself, used to counter No campaign rhetoric throughout.
 e. Legal challenge as legitimacy-building (even in defeat) — the Justice and Equity Centre and HRLC-supported High Court challenges, though unsuccessful, publicly demonstrated the campaign's commitment to legal process, generated media coverage that framed the survey as contested and potentially harmful, and built institutional credibility with audiences who respond to legal and rights-based arguments
